quickStudy.summary:
The Israelites have been in Egypt for hundreds of years since Joseph's time, and they've grown into a huge nation. A new Pharaoh comes to power who doesn't know or care about Joseph — he just sees a massive population of foreigners and gets scared they'll turn on him. So he enslaves them and makes their lives miserable with forced labor. When that doesn't stop them from growing, he orders the Hebrew midwives to kill every baby boy at birth. Two midwives — Shiphrah and Puah — refuse because they fear God more than Pharaoh. So Pharaoh escalates and tells ALL his people to throw Hebrew baby boys into the Nile.

This is where Moses enters the story. His mom hides him for three months, then puts him in a waterproofed basket in the river. Pharaoh's own daughter finds the baby, feels compassion, and raises him as her grandson — in Pharaoh's palace. Moses grows up with every privilege, but when he sees an Egyptian beating a Hebrew slave, he kills the Egyptian. Word gets out, and Moses has to flee to Midian. He spends years as a shepherd, marries Zipporah, and seems to settle into a quiet life.

Then everything changes. God appears in a burning bush that doesn't burn up. He tells Moses He's seen His people suffering and He's going to deliver them — through Moses. Moses pushes back hard: 'Who am I to do this? They won't believe me. I can't even speak well.' God answers every objection and finally sends Aaron to help. Moses goes back to Egypt, confronts Pharaoh with 'Let my people go,' and Pharaoh responds by making the slaves' work even harder. The Israelites turn on Moses, and Moses turns to God asking why He sent him if things are just getting worse. God reassures him: 'I am the LORD. I will bring them out.'

mustRead.why: Two ordinary women — midwives — stand up to the most powerful ruler on earth because they fear God more than Pharaoh. This is one of the first acts of civil disobedience in scripture, and God blesses them for it.
The burning bush — God calls Moses by name, reveals Himself as 'I AM,' and commissions an insecure shepherd to change history. This is THE calling story of the Old Testament.
Moses gives God every excuse in the book for why he can't do what God is asking. If any of the boys have ever felt inadequate or underqualified, this is their passage.
Moses does what God asked and things immediately get WORSE. He's honest with God about his frustration, and God responds with one of the most powerful covenant promises in scripture.

quotableVerses.text: But the midwives feared God, and did not as the king of Egypt commanded them, but saved the men children alive.
And he said, Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground.
And Moses said unto God, Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh, and that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt? And he said, Certainly I will be with thee.
And God said unto Moses, I AM THAT I AM: and he said, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, I AM hath sent me unto you.
Now therefore go, and I will be with thy mouth, and teach thee what thou shalt say.

Wherefore say unto the children of Israel, I am the LORD, and I will bring you out from under the burdens of the Egyptians, and I will rid you out of their bondage, and I will redeem you with a stretched out arm, and with great judgments: And I will take you to me for a people, and I will be to you a God.

quickStudy.context:
When Exodus opens, roughly 400 years have passed since Joseph saved Egypt from famine. The Israelites entered Egypt as a family of about 70 people; they've now grown into a nation of possibly over a million. The 'new king which knew not Joseph' may refer to a new Egyptian dynasty — possibly the Hyksos being overthrown and native Egyptian rulers returning to power, who had no loyalty to Joseph's legacy. The slavery described isn't random cruelty — the Israelites were forced to build 'treasure cities' (supply cities) like Pithom and Raamses, massive construction projects that required enormous labor forces. This was state-sponsored oppression at an industrial scale.

The burning bush moment is loaded with significance. Mount Horeb (also called Sinai) is where Moses will later receive the Ten Commandments — God is already drawing Moses to this sacred place. When God says 'I AM THAT I AM' (Hebrew: 'Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh'), He's revealing His covenant name — a name so sacred that Jews to this day won't speak it aloud. It means something like 'I Am the One Who Is' or 'I Will Be What I Will Be' — God is eternal, self-existent, and unchanging. He's telling Moses: 'I don't depend on anything or anyone. I simply AM.' That's the God who's backing Moses up.

quickInsight: God didn't call Moses because Moses was ready — He called Moses and then made him ready. The same is true for you.
keyThemes: Standing up for what's right even when authority pressures you to do wrong (the midwives vs. Pharaoh)
Feeling inadequate for what God or others are asking of you — and going anyway
God sees your struggles even when it feels like nobody notices
Doing the right thing doesn't always make life easier right away — sometimes it gets harder first
God's identity and promises: 'I AM' — He is constant and He keeps His covenants
discussionQuestions: The midwives had to choose between obeying the most powerful man in the world or obeying God. When have you been in a situation where doing the right thing meant going against what someone with authority or popularity was telling you to do?

Moses gave God a list of reasons he wasn't the right person for the job — 'Who am I? They won't believe me. I can't speak well enough.' What excuses do we make when we feel prompted to do something hard — like inviting someone to church, standing up for someone, or accepting a calling?

After Moses finally obeys and goes to Pharaoh, things get WORSE for the Israelites, not better. Have you ever done the right thing and had it blow up in your face? How do you keep going when obedience doesn't seem to be 'working'?

God told Moses, 'Certainly I will be with thee.' He didn't remove the hard thing — He promised to be there through it. How is that different from what we sometimes expect God to do when we pray for help?

God says 'I AM THAT I AM' — He doesn't change. In a world where friends, trends, algorithms, and even your own feelings change constantly, what does it mean to have something — or Someone — that never changes?
teachingIdeas: The Résumé Exercise: Have the young men write a quick 'résumé' for Moses at the time God called him — fugitive, murderer, speech problems, shepherd in the middle of nowhere. Then ask: 'Would you hire this guy to free a nation?' The point lands fast — God doesn't recruit from a highlight reel. He sees potential we don't. Tie it to priesthood callings, mission calls, or even just being asked to give a talk.

The Burning Bush Shoes-Off Moment: Bring a pair of shoes as a prop. Read Exodus 3:5 and talk about what it means to stand on holy ground. Ask: where is your 'holy ground'? It could be the temple, but it could also be a quiet moment of prayer, a time serving someone, or a conversation where you felt the Spirit. God showed up for Moses in the middle of an ordinary workday — tending sheep. He shows up for us in ordinary moments too.

The 'It Got Worse' Timeline: Draw a simple timeline on the board. Show how Moses obeys → goes to Pharaoh → Pharaoh makes things harder → Israelites are angry at Moses → Moses is frustrated with God. Ask the quorum if they can think of examples from their own lives, from scripture, or from Church history where things got harder right after someone chose to follow God. Then show how the story eventually leads to the Red Sea and total deliverance. Sometimes the hardest chapter comes right before the breakthrough.

Two-Chair Debate — Moses vs. His Excuses: Put two chairs facing each other. Have one young man play 'Moses making excuses' and another play 'God answering.' Read through Exodus 3:11–4:13 together and let them improvise the back-and-forth in modern language. It gets funny and real fast, and they'll remember that God had an answer for every single objection.
activities: Anonymous Courage Cards: Give each young man an index card. Have them write down (anonymously) one situation where it's hard to stand up for what's right — at school, online, with friends. Collect them, read them aloud, and as a quorum brainstorm how to handle each one. This mirrors the midwives' courage and makes it practical. End by challenging each of them to be a 'Shiphrah or Puah' this week — one moment of quiet courage.

Burning Bush Hike or Walk: If weather allows, do a quorum activity outdoors — a hike, a walk, or even just sit outside. Talk about how Moses encountered God in the wilderness, not in a palace. Give each young man 5 minutes of quiet to sit apart and think about (or pray about) one thing God might be asking them to do that feels too big. Regroup and share if they're comfortable. Simple but powerful.

Service Blitz — 'I Have Seen Their Affliction': God told Moses 'I have surely seen the affliction of my people' (Exodus 3:7). Challenge the quorum to be God's eyes and hands this week. Plan a quick service project — yard work for an elderly neighbor, writing notes to someone going through a hard time, or helping a family in the ward. The connection: God sees people who are struggling, and He often sends US to help.
